findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
📝 Pattern: `="),a=(t={},u.forEach((function(e,r){return t[e]=r...`
📦 In function: `decodeText` (1 callers)
🎯 User input detected in context
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: JWT verification is disabled
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 9 co-located issues] Signature verification is disabled. Tokens are accepted without validation.

**Context:**
📦 In function: `_verifyPositiveIntegers` (1 callers)
🔐 Part of authentication flow
findings.suggested_fix:
Always verify JWT signatures:

```python
# Never do this:
# jwt.decode(token, options={'verify_signature': False})

# Always verify:
jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=['RS256'])
```
findings.why_it_matters: JWT vulnerabilities can allow attackers to forge authentication tokens, impersonate users, escalate privileges, or bypass authorization entirely.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 2 functions in call cycle (complexity 43)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 2 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/default-plugins/layout-manager/src/screens/layout_list/mod.rs::impl<LayoutListScreen>::handle_key:51 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/default-plugins/layout-manager/src/screens/layout_list/mod.rs::impl<LayoutListScreen>::handle_typing_filter_mode:136. Aggregate complexity: 43.
findings.suggested_fix: Consider refactoring to eliminate direct mutual calls. Common strategies: merge the two functions, use a callback parameter, or introduce a shared data structure that both functions operate on.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: God class detected: i
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 6 co-located issues] Class 'i' shows signs of being a god class: very high method count (34).

**Role Analysis:** Application — Standard application class

**Metrics:**
- Methods: 34
- Total complexity: 1
- Lines of code: 1

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 34 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=0, p95=1, mean=0.7, n=1226
findings.suggested_fix:
Refactor 'i' to reduce its responsibilities:
**Note:** This class was identified as Application (Standard application class). Thresholds adjusted accordingly.

1. **Extract related methods into separate classes**
- Look for method groups that work with the same data
- Create focused classes with single responsibilities

**Apply SOLID principles:**
- Single Responsibility: Each class should have one reason to change
- Open/Closed: Extend behavior without modifying existing code
- Interface Segregation: Create specific interfaces
- Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions
findings.why_it_matters: God classes violate the Single Responsibility Principle. They are difficult to understand, test, and maintain. Changes to one part may unexpectedly affect other parts, leading to bugs and technical debt.

findings.title: XSS: innerHTML assignment
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] **Direct innerHTML assignment can lead to XSS vulnerabilities**

**Location**: zellij-client/assets/modals.js:348

**Code snippet**:
```
modal.innerHTML = `
```

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is included
in web pages without proper validation or escaping. Attackers can inject malicious
scripts that:
- Steal user session cookies
- Capture keystrokes and credentials
- Redirect users to malicious sites
- Deface the application

This vulnerability is classified as **CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of
Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')**.
findings.why_it_matters: XSS vulnerabilities allow attackers to execute scripts in users' browsers, potentially stealing sensitive data or hijacking user sessions.

findings.title: Path traversal via path.join with user input
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 9 co-located issues] path.join() with user input does NOT prevent path traversal. Joining '/base' with '../etc/passwd' results in '/etc/passwd'.
findings.why_it_matters: path.join() is commonly misunderstood as safe, but it preserves '../' sequences allowing directory escape.

findings.title: Path traversal via string concatenation
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 9 co-located issues] File path constructed via string concatenation with user input. This is vulnerable to directory traversal attacks.
findings.suggested_fix: Use secure path functions and validate the final resolved path is within the allowed directory.
findings.why_it_matters: String concatenation provides no protection against '../' sequences in user input.

findings.title: Potential path traversal in file operation
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 9 co-located issues] File operation with user-controlled input detected. An attacker could use '../' sequences to access files outside the intended directory.
findings.suggested_fix:
1. Use path.basename() to extract filename only
2. Validate resolved path is within allowed directory
3. Use a whitelist of allowed filenames if possible
findings.why_it_matters: Attackers could read sensitive files like /etc/passwd or overwrite critical system files.

findings.title: Insecure TLS/Certificate Validation (rust)
findings.description:
**Certificate validation disabled (reqwest)** (CWE-295)

Disabled certificate or hostname verification detected.

**Pattern**: `danger_accept_invalid_certs(true)`
**File**: zellij-client/src/remote_attach/websockets.rs:97
**Code**: `.danger_accept_invalid_certs(true)`

This allows man-in-the-middle attacks. An attacker on the network can intercept and modify traffic without detection.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `danger_accept_invalid_certs(true)`. For self-signed certs, add the CA to the client builder via `add_root_certificate()`.

findings.title: Insecure TLS/Certificate Validation (rust)
findings.description:
**Hostname validation disabled (reqwest)** (CWE-295)

Disabled certificate or hostname verification detected.

**Pattern**: `danger_accept_invalid_hostnames(true)`
**File**: zellij-client/src/remote_attach/websockets.rs:98
**Code**: `.danger_accept_invalid_hostnames(true)`

This allows man-in-the-middle attacks. An attacker on the network can intercept and modify traffic without detection.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `danger_accept_invalid_certs(true)`. For self-signed certs, add the CA to the client builder via `add_root_certificate()`.

Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/zellij-server/src/screen.rs::impl<Screen>::replace_pane:3651 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/zellij-server/src/tab/mod.rs::impl<Tab>::extract_pane:3875 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/zellij-server/src/tab/mod.rs::impl<Tab>::replace_pane_with_suppressed_pane:3979 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/zellij-server/src/tab/mod.rs::impl<Tab>::suppress_pane:5081 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/zellij-server/src/tab/mod.rs::impl<Tab>::suppress_pane_and_replace_with_pid:2095. Aggregate complexity: 60.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Long method: screen_thread_main (4061 lines)
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function 'screen_thread_main' has 4061 lines (effective threshold: 150).

**Graph Analysis:**
Calls 5 different modules - possible split points
findings.suggested_fix:
This function calls 5 different modules. Consider extracting:
  - `handle_unit()` for unit operations
  - `handle_src()` for src operations
  - `handle_tiled_panes()` for tiled_panes operations
findings.why_it_matters: Long methods are hard to understand, test, and maintain. Each function should do one thing well.

findings.title: God class detected: Pane
findings.description:
Class 'Pane' shows signs of being a god class: very high method count (158).

**Role Analysis:** Application — Standard application class

**Metrics:**
- Methods: 158
- Total complexity: 1
- Lines of code: 416

📊 Threshold: 20 (adaptive) | Actual: 158 | Default would be: 20 | Profile: p90=0, p95=1, mean=0.7, n=1226
findings.suggested_fix:
Refactor 'Pane' to reduce its responsibilities:
**Note:** This class was identified as Application (Standard application class). Thresholds adjusted accordingly.

1. **Extract related methods into separate classes**
- Look for method groups that work with the same data
- Create focused classes with single responsibilities

**Apply SOLID principles:**
- Single Responsibility: Each class should have one reason to change
- Open/Closed: Extend behavior without modifying existing code
- Interface Segregation: Create specific interfaces
- Dependency Inversion: Depend on abstractions
findings.why_it_matters: God classes violate the Single Responsibility Principle. They are difficult to understand, test, and maintain. Changes to one part may unexpectedly affect other parts, leading to bugs and technical debt.

findings.title: Mutual recursion: 4 functions in call cycle (complexity 60)
findings.description: Mutual recursion detected: 4 functions form a call cycle. Cycle: /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/zellij-utils/src/kdl/kdl_layout_parser.rs::impl<KdlLayoutParser<'a>>::insert_children_to_pane_template:647 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/zellij-utils/src/kdl/kdl_layout_parser.rs::impl<KdlLayoutParser<'a>>::parse_child_pane_nodes_for_pane:1262 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/zellij-utils/src/kdl/kdl_layout_parser.rs::impl<KdlLayoutParser<'a>>::parse_pane_node:523 -> /tmp/nix-shell-540336-2497635915/tmp.zZnau8nCIx/zellij/zellij-utils/src/kdl/kdl_layout_parser.rs::impl<KdlLayoutParser<'a>>::parse_pane_node_with_template:712. Aggregate complexity: 60.
findings.suggested_fix: Break the cycle by extracting shared logic into a common helper, introducing an event system, or restructuring the call chain to be unidirectional.
findings.why_it_matters: Mutual recursion creates tight coupling between functions, making them impossible to understand, test, or refactor independently. It can also cause stack overflows if the recursion depth is unbounded.

findings.title: Circular dependency: 3 files
findings.description:
Mutual dependency between: file_list_view.rs ↔ search_view.rs ↔ state.rs

**Weakest link to break:** `file_list_view.rs` → `search_view.rs` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `file_list_view.rs` → `search_view.rs` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `file_list_view.rs` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `search_view.rs`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For small cycles:**
1. Merge tightly coupled modules if they're always used together
2. Use TYPE_CHECKING (Python) or type-only imports (TS) for type hints
3. Extract common code to a third module
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: High panic density in `run_proto_codegen`: 4 panic-family calls
findings.description: Function `run_proto_codegen` contains 4 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 138, line 147, line 148, line 170. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Hidden regex in loop: decodeText
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] Function 'decodeText' contains a loop and calls 'decodeText' which compiles a regex.

**Call chain:** decodeText → a

This may cause regex compilation on every iteration.
findings.suggested_fix:
Options:
1. Cache the compiled regex (lazy_static, OnceLock)
2. Pass pre-compiled regex as parameter
3. Restructure to compile once before loop
findings.why_it_matters: Hidden regex compilation across function boundaries is harder to spot but equally impactful.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
[COMPOUND: 3 co-located issues] Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 1).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.

findings.title: High panic density in `pixel_dimensions_from_bytes`: 4 panic-family calls
findings.description: Function `pixel_dimensions_from_bytes` contains 4 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 212, line 213, line 221, line 222. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Regex compiled inside loop
findings.description:
Regex compiled in loop (loop starts line 2095).

**Performance impact:** Regex compilation is expensive. In a loop of N iterations, this costs N compilations instead of 1.
findings.suggested_fix:
Move regex compilation outside the loop:
```rust
let re = Regex::new(pattern)?;  // Before loop
for item in items {
re.is_match(item);  // Reuse
}
```
findings.why_it_matters: Regex compilation involves parsing and optimization. Doing it once instead of N times can dramatically improve performance.
